Over $365,60037%$98,334.75

            
          

        


        
        

          
Head of Household (HoH)

          

            
Taxable IncomeRateTax on Lower Bracket

            
              
$0 - $16,55010%$0

              
$16,551 - $63,10012%$1,655

              
$63,101 - $100,50022%$7,241

              
$100,501 - $191,95024%$15,469

              
$191,951 - $243,70032%$37,417

              
$243,701 - $609,35035%$53,977

              
Over $609,35037%$181,954.75

            
          

        


        
        

          
Trust & Estate (2024)

          

            
Taxable IncomeRateTax on Lower Bracket

            
              
$0 - $3,10010%$0

              
$3,101 - $11,15024%$310

              
$11,151 - $15,20035%$2,242

              
Over $15,20037%$3,659.50

            
          

        


        
        

          

            
Capital Gains Rates

            

              
RateSingle ThresholdMFJ Threshold

              
                
0%Up to $47,025Up to $94,050

                
15%$47,026 - $518,900$94,051 - $583,750

                
20%Over $518,900Over $583,750

              
            

          

          

            
Self-Employment Tax

            

              Total rate: 15.3%

              Social Security: 12.4% (on first $168,600)

              Medicare: 2.9% (no cap)

              Additional Medicare: 0.9% (over $200K single / $250K MFJ)
            

          

        

      


      
      

        
Legal Requirements & Compliance


        
        

          
Filing Thresholds by Status (2024)

          

            
Filing StatusUnder 6565 or Older

            
              
Single$14,600$16,550

              
Head of Household$21,900$23,850

              
Married Filing Jointly$29,200$30,750 - $32,300

              
Married Filing Separately$5$5

              
Qualifying Surviving Spouse$29,200$30,750

            
          

        


        
        

          
Key Deadlines

          

            
April 15: Individual (1040), C-Corp (1120), Trust/Estate (1041)

            
March 15: S-Corp (1120-S), Partnership (1065)

            
October 15: Extended individual returns

            
September 15: Extended S-Corp/Partnership returns

            
January 31: W-2s and 1099-NEC due

            
Quarterly Est.: 4/15, 6/15, 9/15, 1/15

          

        


        
        

          
Statute of Limitations

          

            3-Year General Rule: IRS must assess tax within 3 years of filing date (IRC 6501(a))

            6-Year Substantial Omission: Extends to 6 years if >25% of gross income omitted (IRC 6501(e))

            Unlimited for Fraud: No statute of limitations for fraudulent returns or failure to file (IRC 6501(c))

            Refund Claims: 3 years from filing or 2 years from payment, whichever is later (IRC 6511)
          

        


        
        

          
Penalty Rates

          

            

              Failure to File (IRC 6651(a)(1)):

              5% per month (up to 25% max)

              Minimum penalty: lesser of $485 or 100% of tax due
            

            

              Failure to Pay (IRC 6651(a)(2)):

              0.5% per month (up to 25% max)

              Reduced to 0.25%/mo if on installment agreement
            

            

              Accuracy-Related (IRC 6662):

              20% of underpayment (negligence, substantial understatement, valuation misstatement)
            

            

              Fraud (IRC 6663):

              75% of underpayment attributable to fraud
            

          

        


        
        

          
Interest Computation

          

            Underpayment Rate: Federal short-term rate + 3% (individual/corporate)

            Overpayment Rate: Federal short-term rate + 3% (individual); FSR + 2% (corporate)

            Large Corporate Underpayment: FSR + 5%

            Compounding: Daily compounding per IRC 6622
          

        

      


      
      

        
Interest & Refund Calculations


        
        

          
IRS Interest Rates (Q1 2024)

          

            
CategoryRateBasis

            
              
Individual Overpayment8%FSR + 3%

              
Corporate Overpayment ($10K+)6.5%FSR + 2%

              
Corporate Overpayment (first $10K)8%FSR + 3%

              
Underpayment8%FSR + 3%

              
Large Corporate Underpayment10%FSR + 5%

            
          

        


        
        

          
Daily Compounding (IRC 6622)

          

            IRS interest is compounded daily. Formula: Interest = Principal x ((1 + rate/365)^days - 1)

            Interest accrues from the due date of the return (not the filing date) until refund is paid.

            For underpayments, interest runs from the due date of the return to the payment date.
          

        


        
        

          
Interest Computation Calculator

          

            

              
              
            

            

              
              
            

            

              
              
            

          

          
          

        


        
        

          
Form 843 - Claim for Refund & Request for Abatement

          

            When to use: Claim refund of assessed penalties, interest, or other amounts (not income tax overpayments -- use 1040-X for those).


            Key Fields:

            Line 1: Your name and SSN/EIN

            Line 2: Spouse's SSN (if joint return)

            Line 3: Amount to be refunded or abated

            Line 4: Tax period(s) involved

            Line 5a: Type of tax (income, employment, estate, etc.)

            Line 5b: Type of penalty (if applicable)

            Line 7: Detailed explanation of why refund/abatement is warranted


            Filing: Mail to the IRS service center where your return was filed. No e-file option.

            Deadline: Same as refund claim deadlines (3 years from filing or 2 years from payment).
          

        


        
        

          
⚡ Advanced Interest Refund Calculator (10 Types)


          
          

            
            
          


          
          

            

              
              
            

            

              
              
            

            

              
              
            

          


          
          

            

              
              
            

            

              
              
            

            

              
              
            

          


          
          

            

              
              
            

            

              
              
            

            

              
              
            

          


          
          

            

              
              
            

            

              
              
            

            

              
              
            

          


          
          

            

              
              
            

            

              
              
            

            

              
              
            

          


          
          

            
            
            
            
          


          
          


          
          

            
Interest Documentation Checklist

            

              
              
              
              
              
              
              
              
              
              
            

          

        

      


      
      

        
1099 / 1065 Refund Mechanics


        
        

          
Your 1099 Filings Summary

          

            Select this tab to load summary...
          

        


        
        

          
How 1099 Income Flows to Returns

          

            1099-NEC (Nonemployee Compensation): Reported on Schedule C (sole proprietor) or business return. Subject to self-employment tax (15.3%).

            1099-MISC (Miscellaneous): Rents (Sch E), royalties (Sch E), other income (Sch 1 Line 8z).

            1099-INT (Interest): Reported on Schedule B if over $1,500. Taxed as ordinary income.

            1099-DIV (Dividends): Qualified dividends taxed at capital gains rates. Ordinary dividends taxed as ordinary income.

            1099-B (Broker Transactions): Reported on Form 8949 and Schedule D. Short-term vs. long-term treatment.

            1099-R (Retirement): Pension/annuity distributions reported on Lines 5a-5b of Form 1040.
          

        


        
        

          
K-1 Pass-Through Mechanics

          

            Partnership (Form 1065 / Schedule K-1):

            Box 1: Ordinary business income (Schedule E, Part II)

            Box 2: Net rental real estate income (Schedule E)

            Box 4a/4b: Guaranteed payments (ordinary income)

            Box 9a: Net long-term capital gain (Schedule D)

            Box 11: Section 179 deduction

            Box 13: Credits and credit recapture


            S-Corp (Form 1120-S / Schedule K-1):

            Box 1: Ordinary business income (Schedule E, Part II)

            Box 2: Net rental real estate income

            Box 7: Net short-term capital gain (Schedule D)

            Box 8a: Net long-term capital gain (Schedule D)

            Box 10: Section 179 deduction

            Box 12: Credits


            Basis Limitation: Losses limited to partner/shareholder basis. Excess carries forward indefinitely.
          

        


        
        

          
Self-Employment Tax on 1099 Income

          

            Who pays: Self-employed individuals with net earnings of $400 or more.

            Rate: 15.3% (12.4% SS + 2.9% Medicare) on 92.35% of net SE income.

            Deduction: 50% of SE tax is deductible on Schedule 1, Line 15.

            Forms: Schedule SE (Self-Employment Tax), attached to Form 1040.
          

        


        
        

          
Quarterly Estimated Payments (Form 1040-ES)

          

            Who must pay: Taxpayers who expect to owe $1,000+ when filing (after withholding and credits).

            Safe harbor: Pay 100% of prior year tax (110% if AGI > $150K) or 90% of current year tax.

            Due dates: Q1: April 15, Q2: June 15, Q3: September 15, Q4: January 15 (next year).

            Penalty: IRC 6654 underpayment penalty calculated quarterly using IRS interest rate.

            Annualized income method: Form 2210 Schedule AI -- useful for uneven income throughout the year.
          

        

      


      
      

        
Financial Instruments & Tax Treatment


        
        

          
Bond Premium Amortization (IRC 171)

          

            Definition: When you buy a bond for more than its face value, the excess is "bond premium."

            Election: You may elect to amortize premium over the bond's remaining life using the constant yield method.

            Tax Effect: Amortized premium reduces your interest income each year. For taxable bonds, it offsets interest; for tax-exempt bonds, it reduces basis only.

            Basis Adjustment: Each year's amortization reduces your adjusted basis in the bond.

            Reporting: Report on Schedule B; adjustment shown on 1099-INT, Box 11.
          

        


        
        

          
Original Issue Discount (OID) Adjustments

          

            Definition: The difference between a bond's face value and its issue price when issued at a discount.

            Inclusion: OID is included in income annually using the constant yield method, even if no cash received (phantom income).

            De minimis rule: OID is disregarded if less than 0.25% x face value x years to maturity.

            Reporting: Form 1099-OID issued by payer; report on Schedule B.

            Basis increase: Each year's OID inclusion increases your adjusted basis.
          

        


        
        

          
Wash Sale Rules (IRC 1091)

          

            Rule: You cannot deduct a loss on sale of stock or securities if you buy "substantially identical" securities within 30 days before or after the sale.

            30-Day Window: The wash sale window is 61 days total (30 before + sale date + 30 after).

            Disallowed loss: Added to the basis of the replacement security, effectively deferring the loss.

            Holding period: The holding period of the sold security tacks onto the replacement security.

            Reporting: Form 8949 with Code W in Column (f). Brokers may report on 1099-B.
          

        


        
        

          
Like-Kind Exchanges (IRC 1031)

          

            Scope: Only real property held for business or investment qualifies (post-TCJA 2017). Personal property no longer eligible.

            45-Day Rule: Replacement property must be identified within 45 days of sale.

            180-Day Rule: Exchange must be completed within 180 days of sale (or tax return due date, if earlier).

            Boot: Any non-like-kind property received (cash, debt relief) is taxable.

            Basis: Carryover basis from relinquished property, adjusted for boot and gain recognized.

            Reporting: Form 8824 (Like-Kind Exchanges) attached to return.
          

        


        
        

          
Installment Sales (IRC 453)

          

            Definition: A sale where at least one payment is received after the tax year of the sale.

            Gross profit percentage: (Selling price - adjusted basis) / selling price. This % is applied to each installment received.

            Exclusions: Dealer dispositions, inventory, publicly traded property are generally excluded.

            Interest charge: IRC 453A imposes interest on deferred tax if obligation exceeds $5M (non-farm) or $150K (dealer).

            Electing out: You can elect out of installment method by reporting full gain in year of sale.

            Reporting: Form 6252 (Installment Sale Income) each year payments are received.
          

        


        
        

          
⚡ Interactive Financial Instrument Loss Calculator


          
          

            

              
Method 1: Realized Loss (§165)

              
Sold security at a loss — Form 8949 / Schedule D

            

            

              
Method 2: Worthlessness (§165(g))

              
Security became totally worthless

            

            

              
Method 3: Mark-to-Market (§475)

              
Trader election — ordinary loss treatment

            

            

              
Method 4: Hedging (§988/§1256)

              
Currency / commodity hedge transactions

FEDERAL DAMAGES & RESTITUTION FRAMEWORK

        
Complete legal framework documenting the unrepealed federal monetary system from 1775–1913, civilian prosecution rights, federal prosecution triggers, and modern day application

        
      


      
      

        
PART I — FOUNDATIONAL FEDERAL MONETARY FRAMEWORK (1775–1913)

        ▼
      

      


        
        

          

            §1 — Birth of Federal Par-Value Doctrine (1775)
            ▼
          

          

            
Congress established the nation's first monetary requirement on June 22, 1775, creating mandatory par acceptance of federal bills. This resolution required all officers and citizens to accept Continental Notes as equal in value to the same sum in Spanish milled dollars, meaning par-value acceptance existed BEFORE the Constitution itself. This statute required officers and citizens to accept federal bills exactly at face value, and these principles have never been repealed.

            
Congressional Record: Journals of the Continental Congress, Vol. II, June 22, 1775

            
Act of Congress: Resolution Authorizing the Emission of Bills of Credit

            
Statute at Large: Journals of the Continental Congress, 1775

            
U.S. Code: 31 U.S.C. § 5103

            
Supreme Court Precedent 1: Juilliard v. Greenman, 110 U.S. 421 (1884) — held that Congress's authority over currency descends from the same monetary power exercised by the Continental Congress

            
Supreme Court Precedent 2: Knox v. Lee, 79 U.S. (12 Wall.) 457 (1871) — affirmed that Congress may declare instruments legal tender at their declared value, establishing continuity from 1775 to present

            

              
⚠️ REBUTTAL TO CONTRARY PRESUMPTION: Any presumption that the par-value doctrine was merely wartime policy is REBUTTED by the Supreme Court's recognition in Juilliard that Congress's monetary authority descends from 1775 and remains continuous. Modern claims that these laws are 'outdated' or 'superseded' fail because Congress has NEVER expressly repealed these statutes.

            

          

        


        
        

          

            §2 — The 1786 Ordinance Mandating Par Acceptance
            ▼
          

          

            
Congress under the Articles of Confederation mandated that all federal notes and certificates issued for public credit 'shall pass at their nominal value,' meaning par value. This statute required every federal fiscal agent to receive United States notes, certificates, and bills 'at their denomination without discount,' establishing the par-value doctrine as federal law BEFORE the U.S. Constitution.

            
Congressional Record: Journals of the Confederation Congress, May 4, 1786

            
Act of Congress: Ordinance for the Regulation of the Treasury Department

            
Statute at Large: Journals of the Confederation Congress, 1786

            
U.S. Code: 31 U.S.C. § 5118

            
Supreme Court Precedent 1: Hollingsworth v. Virginia, 3 U.S. (3 Dall.) 378 (1798) — recognized that pre-constitutional congressional financial enactments remain valid unless repealed by Congress

            
Supreme Court Precedent 2: Faw v. Marsteller, 6 U.S. (2 Cranch) 10 (1804) — confirmed that pre-constitutional debt and currency laws retain binding force unless expressly repealed

            

              
⚠️ REBUTTAL TO CONTRARY PRESUMPTION: The presumption that constitutional adoption nullified prior monetary laws is REBUTTED. The Supreme Court in Hollingsworth explicitly held Confederation-era financial laws remain binding unless expressly repealed. No such repeal has ever occurred.

            

          

        


        
        

          

            §3 — Coinage Act of 1792 (First Constitutional Par-Value Statute)
            ▼
          

          

            
Congress enacted the Coinage Act requiring U.S. coins to be accepted at exact par value 'according to their denomination.' This Act formally established that federal monetary instruments must be honored at their declared value and created the national doctrine of nondiscountable federal money.

            
Congressional Record: 1 Cong. Rec. (1792)

            
Act of Congress: Coinage Act of April 2, 1792

            
Statute at Large: 1 Stat. 246

            
U.S. Code: 31 U.S.C. § 5112

            
Supreme Court Precedent 1: Craig v. Missouri, 29 U.S. (4 Pet.) 410 (1830) — held that only Congress may establish instruments that must be accepted at their declared value

            
Supreme Court Precedent 2: Darrington v. Bank of Alabama, 54 U.S. (13 How.) 12 (1851) — reaffirmed that par-value requirements imposed by Congress must be enforced as written

            

              
⚠️ REBUTTAL TO CONTRARY PRESUMPTION: The presumption that states may establish alternative monetary valuations is REBUTTED. Craig v. Missouri definitively held that ONLY Congress may establish monetary value requirements.

            

          

        


        
        

          

            §4 — Legal Tender Act (1862)
            ▼
          

          

            
Congress declared United States notes 'lawful money and a legal tender in payment of all debts, public and private.' This Act established that all federal currency must be accepted at face value and created the legal mechanism for debt extinguishment through lawful money tender.

            
Congressional Record: 37 Cong. Rec. (1862)

            
Act of Congress: Legal Tender Act of February 25, 1862

            
Statute at Large: 12 Stat. 345

            
U.S. Code: 31 U.S.C. § 5103

            
Supreme Court Precedent 1: Knox v. Lee, 79 U.S. (12 Wall.) 457 (1871) — affirmed Congress's power to make United States notes legal tender for all debts

            
Supreme Court Precedent 2: Juilliard v. Greenman, 110 U.S. 421 (1884) — held the legal tender power is permanent, not limited to wartime, and Congress may exercise it at any time

            

              
⚠️ REBUTTAL TO CONTRARY PRESUMPTION: The presumption that the Legal Tender Act was a wartime emergency measure is REBUTTED. Juilliard v. Greenman explicitly held the legal tender power is PERMANENT and not limited to wartime conditions.

            

          

        


        
        

          

            §5 — National Banking Act (1863) & Coinage Act (1873)
            ▼
          

          

            
Congress created the national banking system requiring all national banks to accept United States notes at par value. The subsequent Coinage Act of 1873 further codified federal monetary standards. Together these Acts established that national banks operate under federal par-value requirements and cannot discount federal instruments.

            
Congressional Record: 37 Cong. Rec. (1863); 42 Cong. Rec. (1873)

            
Act of Congress: National Banking Act of February 25, 1863; Coinage Act of February 12, 1873

            
Statute at Large: 12 Stat. 665; 17 Stat. 424

            
U.S. Code: 12 U.S.C. § 38; 31 U.S.C. § 5112

            
Supreme Court Precedent 1: Farmers & Mechanics Bank v. Dearing, 91 U.S. 29 (1875) — held national banks are federal instrumentalities bound by federal monetary law

            
Supreme Court Precedent 2: Davis v. Elmira Savings Bank, 161 U.S. 275 (1896) — reaffirmed that national banking operations are governed exclusively by federal statute

            

              
⚠️ REBUTTAL TO CONTRARY PRESUMPTION: The presumption that national banks may set their own valuation policies is REBUTTED. Farmers & Mechanics Bank v. Dearing held national banks are federal instrumentalities BOUND by federal monetary statutes.

            

          

        


        
        

          

            §6 — Federal Reserve Act (1913)
            ▼
          

          

            
Congress established the par-value requirement for all eligible paper deposited with Federal Reserve Banks. Section 16 (38 Stat. 265) explicitly states that Federal Reserve notes 'shall be redeemed in lawful money on demand,' establishing them as obligations of the United States and defining par treatment upon issuance and deposit. Section 13 designates promissory notes as 'eligible paper' that becomes a federal obligation upon deposit.

            
Congressional Record: 63 Cong. Rec. (1913)

            
Act of Congress: Federal Reserve Act of December 23, 1913

            
Statute at Large: 38 Stat. 251

            
U.S. Code: 12 U.S.C. § 343

            
Supreme Court Precedent 1: Perry v. United States, 294 U.S. 330 (1935) — held that when Congress pledges the credit of the United States for a monetary obligation, that obligation cannot be impaired

            
Supreme Court Precedent 2: Norman v. Baltimore & Ohio R. Co., 294 U.S. 240 (1935) — reaffirmed that Congress's monetary enactments bind all courts without exception

            

              
⚠️ REBUTTAL TO CONTRARY PRESUMPTION: The presumption that the Federal Reserve operates as a 'private' institution exempt from federal monetary requirements is REBUTTED. Perry v. United States held that Federal Reserve obligations are pledges of United States credit that CANNOT BE IMPAIRED.

            

          

        


      


      
      

        
PART II — CIVILIAN RIGHT TO INITIATE PROSECUTION AGAINST PUBLIC OFFICIALS

        ▼
      

      


        
        

          

            §1 — Criminal Liability for Rights Violations (18 U.S.C. § 242)
            ▼
          

          

            
Congress enacted criminal penalties for ANY official who willfully deprives a person of rights under color of law. This creates non-judicial-branch enforcement independent of the accused branch. A civilian whose rights are violated by a public official has the right to seek federal criminal prosecution through the U.S. Attorney under this statute.

            
Congressional Record: 39 Cong. Rec. (1866)

            
Act of Congress: Civil Rights Act of 1866

            
Statute at Large: 14 Stat. 27

            
U.S. Code: 18 U.S.C. § 242

            
Supreme Court Precedent 1: United States v. Price, 383 U.S. 787 (1966) — held that this statute applies to ALL officials, including state judges, and criminal prosecution does NOT require judicial-branch permission

            
Supreme Court Precedent 2: Screws v. United States, 325 U.S. 91 (1945) — affirmed Congress's power to impose criminal liability on officials who violate constitutional rights

            

              
⚠️ REBUTTAL TO CONTRARY PRESUMPTION: The presumption that officials enjoy immunity from criminal prosecution for official acts is REBUTTED. Price held that § 242 applies to ALL officials including judges. Judicial immunity applies only to CIVIL suits, not to federal criminal prosecution.

            

          

        


        
        

          

            §2 — Conspiracy Liability for Multiple Officials (18 U.S.C. § 241)
            ▼
          

          

            
Congress created criminal conspiracy liability for officials acting together to violate civil rights. This makes it a felony for ANY group of officials—judicial, executive, legislative, or administrative—to conspire to oppress or injure a civilian's constitutional rights. Redress does NOT require the accused branch to judge itself.

            
Congressional Record: 41 Cong. Rec. (1870)

            
Act of Congress: Enforcement Act of May 31, 1870

            
Statute at Large: 16 Stat. 140

            
U.S. Code: 18 U.S.C. § 241

            
Supreme Court Precedent 1: United States v. Classic, 313 U.S. 299 (1941) — held that federal criminal jurisdiction applies whenever officials conspire to interfere with rights guaranteed by Congress

            
Supreme Court Precedent 2: Ex parte Yarbrough, 110 U.S. 651 (1884) — upheld Congress's authority to criminally punish officials who violate federal rights

            

              
⚠️ REBUTTAL TO CONTRARY PRESUMPTION: The presumption that 'prosecutorial discretion' shields official conspiracies is REBUTTED. Ex parte Yarbrough held Congress has authority to punish officials who violate federal rights REGARDLESS of the branch involved.

            

          

        


        
        

          

            §3 — Private Attorney General Doctrine & Qui Tam Actions (42 U.S.C. § 1983 / 31 U.S.C. § 3730)
            ▼
          

          

            
Congress established that private citizens may act as attorneys general to enforce federal rights violations under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 and may bring qui tam actions under the False Claims Act (31 U.S.C. § 3730) when the government is being defrauded. These statutes create independent civilian enforcement mechanisms that do not depend on government prosecutors choosing to act.

            
Congressional Record: 42 Cong. Rec. (1871)

            
Act of Congress: Civil Rights Act of 1871 (Ku Klux Klan Act); False Claims Act of 1863

            
Statute at Large: 17 Stat. 13; 12 Stat. 696

            
U.S. Code: 42 U.S.C. § 1983; 31 U.S.C. § 3730

            
Supreme Court Precedent 1: Newman v. Piggie Park Enterprises, 390 U.S. 400 (1968) — established the private attorney general doctrine allowing citizens to enforce federal civil rights

            
Supreme Court Precedent 2: Vermont Agency of Natural Resources v. United States ex rel. Stevens, 529 U.S. 765 (2000) — upheld qui tam standing for private citizens to sue on behalf of the government

            

              
⚠️ REBUTTAL TO CONTRARY PRESUMPTION: The presumption that only government prosecutors may enforce federal rights is REBUTTED. Newman v. Piggie Park established that private citizens serve as 'private attorneys general' with standing to enforce federal statutes independently.

            

          

        


      


      
      

        
PART III — FEDERAL PROSECUTION TRIGGER FRAMEWORK

        ▼
      

      


        
        

          

            §1 — Trigger for Federal Mail Fraud Prosecution (18 U.S.C. § 1341)
            ▼
          

          

            
Congress extinguished enforceability under the Legal Tender Act. Any mailed notice asserting delinquency or foreclosure on an extinguished obligation constitutes a false representation of federal financial status prosecutable as mail fraud.

            
Congressional Record: 37 Cong. Rec. (1862)

            
Act of Congress: Legal Tender Act of February 25, 1862

            
Statute at Large: 12 Stat. 345

            
U.S. Code: 18 U.S.C. § 1341

            
Supreme Court Precedent 1: Durland v. United States, 161 U.S. 306 (1896) — held mail fraud covers all schemes to obtain property by misrepresenting financial obligations

            
Supreme Court Precedent 2: Bridge v. Phoenix Bond, 553 U.S. 639 (2008) — held fraud liability attaches regardless of whether the victim relied on the false claim

            

              
⚠️ REBUTTAL TO CONTRARY PRESUMPTION: The presumption that routine foreclosure notices are protected business communications is REBUTTED. Durland held mail fraud covers ALL schemes misrepresenting financial obligations. A foreclosure notice claiming money is owed when the obligation was extinguished is a federal crime.

            

          

        


        
        

          

            §2 — Trigger for RICO Prosecution (18 U.S.C. § 1962)
            ▼
          

          

            
Congress created extinguishment rules foundational to national monetary integrity. Banks or trustees repeatedly enforcing extinguished obligations constitute racketeering activity subject to RICO prosecution.

            
Congressional Record: 42 Cong. Rec. (1873)

            
Act of Congress: Coinage Act of February 12, 1873

            
Statute at Large: 17 Stat. 424

            
U.S. Code: 18 U.S.C. § 1962

            
Supreme Court Precedent 1: Sedima v. Imrex, 473 U.S. 479 (1985) — held financial fraud schemes qualify as racketeering

            
Supreme Court Precedent 2: H.J. Inc. v. Northwestern Bell, 492 U.S. 229 (1989) — held RICO applies when fraudulent patterns affect property

            

              
⚠️ REBUTTAL TO CONTRARY PRESUMPTION: The presumption that 'legitimate businesses' cannot be RICO enterprises is REBUTTED. Sedima held financial fraud schemes qualify as racketeering REGARDLESS of the entity's otherwise legitimate operations.

            

          

        


      


      
      

        
PART IV — MODERN DAY APPLICATION FOR PROPERTY PROTECTION

        ▼
      

      


        
        

          

            §1 — How Debt Extinguishment Operates Today
            ▼
          

          

            
When a homeowner executes a promissory note to obtain Federal Reserve notes through a Federal Reserve agent bank, that promissory note becomes 'eligible paper' under Section 13 of the Federal Reserve Act. The bank deposits the promissory note with the Federal Reserve and receives Federal Reserve notes at par value, meaning the note is accepted at its full face amount as an obligation of the United States pursuant to the unrepealed statutes dating from 1775 through 1913.

            
Congressional Record: 63 Cong. Rec. (1913)

            
Act of Congress: Federal Reserve Act of December 23, 1913, Section 13

            
Statute at Large: 38 Stat. 251

            
U.S. Code: 12 U.S.C. § 343

            
Supreme Court Precedent 1: Lynch v. United States, 292 U.S. 571 (1934) — held that a property interest including a contractual right is itself 'property' protected by the Constitution

            
Supreme Court Precedent 2: Hodel v. Irving, 481 U.S. 704 (1987) — confirmed that even fractional or intangible property interests are constitutionally protected property

            

              
⚠️ REBUTTAL TO CONTRARY PRESUMPTION: The presumption that the homeowner 'owes' money after signing a promissory note is REBUTTED. The bank received FULL PAR VALUE at the moment of deposit. Lynch held property interests are constitutionally protected—the homeowner's interest in their property cannot be taken based on an extinguished obligation.

            

          

        


      


      
      

        
IRREFUTABLE CONCLUSION OF LAW

        
NONE of the par-value laws from 1775 through 1913 have EVER been repealed by Congress.

        
The complete chronological chain proves that the par-value doctrine was established BEFORE the Constitution, continuously reaffirmed through multiple Acts of Congress, extended to all forms of federal currency and banking instruments, and codified in the Federal Reserve Act as EXISTING law rather than new law.

        
Every Act of Congress cited remains in the Statutes at Large, has been recognized by the Supreme Court as valid and binding, carries forward into the United States Code where not expressly repealed, and establishes that instruments deposited with Federal Reserve agents must be received at par as obligations of the United States.

        
The doctrine that a promissory note or other eligible paper deposited with a Federal Reserve agent is received at par and becomes a federal obligation eliminating any outstanding debt obligation is supported by over 138 years of unrepealed federal legislation and Supreme Court precedent.

      


      
      

        
⚠️ MASTER REBUTTAL TO ALL CONTRARY PRESUMPTIONS


        

          
PRESUMPTION: "These laws are outdated and no longer apply."

          
REBUTTAL: The Supreme Court in the Legal Tender Cases, 110 U.S. 421 (1884), held that federal monetary statutes remain valid UNTIL EXPRESSLY REPEALED BY CONGRESS. No such repeal has occurred. Age alone does not invalidate statutory law.

        


        

          
PRESUMPTION: "Modern banking practices supersede these old statutes."

          
REBUTTAL: Norman v. Baltimore & Ohio R. Co., 294 U.S. 240 (1935), held that Congress's monetary enactments CANNOT BE NULLIFIED by administrative or judicial reinterpretation. Banking "practices" cannot override statutory law.

        


        

          
PRESUMPTION: "The Federal Reserve is a private institution not bound by these laws."

          
REBUTTAL: Perry v. United States, 294 U.S. 330 (1935), held that Federal Reserve obligations are PLEDGES OF UNITED STATES CREDIT. The Federal Reserve operates under federal statute and is bound by all federal monetary requirements.

        


        

          
PRESUMPTION: "State foreclosure law governs mortgage enforcement."

          
REBUTTAL: Gibbons v. Ogden, 22 U.S. 1 (1824), and McCulloch v. Maryland, 17 U.S. 316 (1819), established that federal law SUPERSEDES conflicting state law. State foreclosure statutes cannot override federal par-value requirements.

        


        

          
PRESUMPTION: "Homeowners contractually agreed to pay regardless of these laws."

          
REBUTTAL: Butler v. Horwitz, 74 U.S. 258 (1868), held that congressional monetary declarations SUPERSEDE private contract terms. No contract can waive or modify federal statutory requirements.

        


        

          
PRESUMPTION: "Courts have rejected these arguments before."

          
REBUTTAL: Lower court rulings that contradict Supreme Court precedent are NOT binding and can be challenged. The Supreme Court cases cited herein are controlling authority that lower courts are REQUIRED to follow.

Conversion Error

            

            
Works best with text-based PDFs. Image-based or encrypted PDFs may not convert.

          


          

          
          

            
Notes:

            
• PDF to Markdown: Works with text-based PDFs. Image/scanned PDFs won't convert.

            
• Markdown to PDF: Creates basic formatted PDF from markdown content.

            
• Complex formatting, images, and tables may not preserve perfectly.

            
• All processing happens in your browser — files are never uploaded to a server.

      🛰️ API Scout — Autonomous API Discovery Engine
      

        
        
        
        
      

    

    

      
Queries public API directories, filters for legal/financial/government relevance, tests each endpoint live, and integrates working APIs directly into your agent routing system.

      

        
        
        
      

      

        
        
      

      
      

    

  

  


  
  

  

    

      ⚖️ OpenClaw — Legal & Research API Discovery
      

        
        
        
        
        
      

    

    

      
OpenClaw integrates legal databases, web search (Tavily), web crawling (Firecrawl), and research automation (Apify) into your agent routing. Configure API keys below, then use category buttons to discover and integrate live APIs.


      
      

        

          
🕷️ Apify — Web Scraping & Research

          
SEC EDGAR, Google Search, News, Financial Data. Get key ↗

          
        

        

          
🔍 Tavily — AI-Optimized Web Search

          
Real-time search results optimized for AI agents. Get key ↗

          
          
        

        

          
🔥 Firecrawl — Web Crawling & Extraction

          
Convert any website to clean markdown for agent context. Get key ↗

Enter a search term or click a category above to discover APIs.

LLM Provider Status


Configure your API keys in API Keys to get started.






Quick Start



1. Go to API Keys - select Ollama (Local) for free local AI, or enter a cloud provider key

2. Go to Preferences to set your name, state, and vernacular

3. Go to Chat and ask your question - the system routes to the right agent

4. Upload documents directly into chat for agent analysis

5. Use for voice input and to hear responses read aloud








  
  







  🗄️ Digital File Cabinet
  

    
    
    
  







  
  
  






  
  







  
Not synced yet — click Sync Sheets to load workbook data.

  







  
  










  
  





🏛️ The Dream Team — 600 Attorneys

















Facts and conclusions of law only - Congressional Record, Congressional Acts, Statutes at Large as codification, Supreme Court of the United States precedent.
































  
  




Conversation History





No history yet.







  
  




📖 How-To Guide — The Firm




Getting Started


1. Go to API Keys select Ollama (Local) for free local AI, or enter a cloud provider key (DeepSeek, OpenAI, Claude, xAI, etc.)


2. Go to Chat type your question - auto-routes to the right specialist


3. Use for voice input. Use on any message to hear it spoken.


4. Click in chat to upload documents for agent analysis.


The Firm - Agent Roster


Legal Expert - Constitutional, Commercial, Criminal, Civil. Presumption Killer. 42 years of practice.


Tax Expert - Federal tax, NOLs, assets, IRS defense. Revenue Acts + Statutes at Large.


Arbitration - Federal Arbitration Act 1925, 43 Stat. 883. Contract review, award issuance.


Corporation - Formation, governance, SPVs, religious corps, consolidation, tax elections.


Trust - C.L.O.C.E.S.T., Mega Trust Constitution, constitutional property as trust res.


Accounting - GAAP double-entry, journal entries, financials, tax prep, payment orders.


Code Expert - Full-stack: HTML/CSS/JS, Python, databases, API integration.


Research - Deep analysis with source verification and cross-referencing.


SYPHER Protocol v7.1


Immutable enforcement: Congressional Acts Statutes at Large Supreme Court U.S. Code (parentheses only). No hedging. No disclaimers. No sovereign citizen language. Zero speculation.


Security


All data stored in browser localStorage. Keys sent only to their own API. Password vault: AES-256-GCM + PBKDF2 (600,000 iterations).

🧠 Memory




The agent learns from your conversations. Topics discussed, preferences expressed, and patterns observed are stored locally.

Free LLM API Resources

        

            
From mfoud444/ollamafreeapi and cheahjs/free-llm-api-resources — free and trial-credit LLM API providers. Get a key and add it in Settings to use as a provider or failover.

            
OpenRouter
Free
20 req/min, 50 req/day. 25+ free models incl. DeepSeek R1, Llama 3.3, Qwen.
Get Key
Groq
Free
14,400 req/day, 6K tokens/min. Llama 3.3 70B, Mixtral, Gemma2.
Get Key
Cerebras
Free
30 req/min, 14,400 req/day, 1M tokens/day. Llama 3.3 70B.
Get Key
Google AI Studio
Free
Gemini Flash: 250K tokens/min, 20 req/day.
Get Key
Cloudflare Workers AI
Free
10,000 neurons/day. 60+ models.
Get Key
Cohere
Free
20 req/min, 1,000 req/month. 14 models.
Get Key
GitHub Models
Free
Depends on Copilot tier. 45+ models.
Get Key
NVIDIA NIM
Free
40 req/min. Phone verification required.
Get Key
Mistral
Free
1 req/sec, 500K tokens/min, 1B tokens/month.
Get Key
HuggingFace
Free
$0.10/month free credits. Models under 10GB.
Get Key
OllamaFreeAPI
Free
6 community servers. 16 models (Llama, DeepSeek, Mistral, Qwen).
Get Key
Fireworks
Trial $1
Various open models.
Get Key

Trial $5
16+ models incl. DeepSeek, Llama.
Get Key

20+ models incl. DeepSeek.

Awesome Open Source AI — Curated Directory

        

            
From alvinreal/awesome-opensource-ai — notable open-source AI models, libraries, infrastructure, and developer tools.

            

                
                
                65 of 65 tools
            

            
PyTorch
Frameworks
Dynamic computation graphs, Pythonic API, dominant in research and production.
GitHub
TensorFlow
Frameworks
End-to-end platform with excellent production deployment and TPU support.
GitHub
JAX + Flax
Frameworks
High-performance numerical computing with composable transformations.
GitHub
Keras
Frameworks
High-level API running on multiple backends (TensorFlow, JAX, PyTorch).
GitHub
Burn
Rust ML
Next-gen deep learning framework in Rust. Backend-agnostic with CPU/GPU/WASM.
GitHub
Candle (HF)
Rust ML
Minimalist ML framework for Rust with PyTorch-like API.
GitHub
Transformers (HF)
NLP
De facto standard for pretrained NLP models. 1M+ models available.
GitHub
sentence-transformers
NLP
Classic library for sentence and image embeddings.
GitHub
Polars
Data
Blazing-fast DataFrame library (Rust backend) for large-scale workloads.
GitHub
scikit-learn
Classical ML
Industry-standard library for traditional ML: classification, regression, clustering.
GitHub
XGBoost
Classical ML
Scalable gradient boosting. Dominates Kaggle and tabular competitions.
GitHub
Optuna
AutoML
Modern hyperparameter optimization with pruning and visualizations.
GitHub
AutoGluon
AutoML
AWS AutoML for tabular, image, text, and multimodal data.
GitHub
DeepSpeed
Training
Microsoft deep learning optimization for extreme-scale training (ZeRO, offloading).
GitHub
FlashAttention
Training
Fast exact attention kernels reducing memory and accelerating transformers.
GitHub
PyTorch Lightning
Training
High-level PyTorch wrapper removing boilerplate and adding best practices.
GitHub
Qwen3.5 (Alibaba)
Models
Multimodal open series 0.8B-122B. Strong in coding, math, vision.
GitHub
DeepSeek-V3.2 / R1
Models
MoE family with exceptional reasoning, math, and efficient inference.
GitHub
Gemma 3 (Google)
Models
Lightweight powerful open models with 100+ language support.
GitHub
MiniMax-M2.1
Models
Open-weight model line for long-context reasoning and agentic tasks.
GitHub
Kimi K2.5 (Moonshot)
Models
Frontier open-weight MoE with 256K context, strong coding and reasoning.
GitHub
OLMo 2 (Allen AI)
Models
Fully open-source LLMs (1B-32B) with complete transparency.
GitHub
DeepSeek-Coder-V2
Code Models
Best-in-class open coding model (236B MoE).
GitHub
Qwen3-Coder-Next
Code Models
Leading open coding model for cost-effective agent deployment.
GitHub
StarCoder2
Code Models
15B model trained on 600+ programming languages.
GitHub
Qwen3-VL
Vision
Flagship VLM with 256K context, visual agent capabilities, 3D grounding.
GitHub
InternVL3
Vision
Native multimodal pretraining with superior perception and reasoning.
GitHub
LLaVA-OneVision
Vision
Expanded capabilities across vision-language tasks.
GitHub
Whisper (OpenAI)
Audio
Gold-standard open speech-to-text model.
GitHub
Fish Speech
Audio
Zero-shot TTS with excellent voice cloning.
GitHub

Audio
Frontier voice AI with expressive longform conversational speech synthesis.
GitHub

Video
Fully open video generation model rivaling closed systems.
GitHub
CogVideoX
Video
High-quality open text-to-video model (5B-12B).
GitHub
vLLM
Inference
High-throughput LLM serving with PagedAttention. Production standard.
GitHub
llama.cpp
Inference
CPU/GPU inference in C/C++ with GGUF quantization. Runs anywhere.
GitHub
Ollama
Inference
One-command local model deployment. The Docker of LLMs.
GitHub

Inference
Production-grade serving with continuous batching and tensor parallelism.
GitHub

Inference
Structured generation language for fast, controllable LLM programs.

🔑 API Keys

🔌 Provider API Keys





💾 GCMemory — Persistent Agent Memory


Memory is stored in this browser's IndexedDB engine. No additional API key required. The Claude/provider key above is the only key needed. Agents recall prior legal, financial, and case-specific exchanges across sessions automatically.



✅
ACTIVE - Memory engine online. No key gate.

Return of Organization Exempt From Income Tax

  
Under section 501(c), 527, or 4947(a)(1) of the Internal Revenue Code (except private foundations)

  Do not enter social security numbers on this form as it may be made public.

  Go to www.irs.gov/Form990 for instructions and the latest information.

  

  
  

  

  
A For the 2024 calendar year, or tax year beginning _______, 20__, and ending _______, 20__

  D Employer identification number

  
  

  

  

  

  

  C
  

  
Name of organization

  
  
Doing business as

  
  
Number and street (or P.O. box if mail not delivered to street address)

  
  
City or town, state or province, country, and ZIP or foreign postal code

  
  

  

  

  

  E Telephone number

  


  F Name and address of principal officer

  

  


  G Gross receipts $ 
  

  

  

  H(a) Is this a group return for subordinates?  Yes  No
  H(b) Are all subordinates included?  Yes  No
  I Tax-exempt status:
   501(c)(3)
   501(c)( ) (insert no.)
   4947(a)(1)
   527
  

  
  
Part I Summary

  
1

2

  
Check this box  if the organization discontinued its operations or disposed of more than 25% of its net assets.

  

  

  
3

  
Number of voting members of the governing body (Part VI, line 1a)

  

  

  

  
4

  
Number of independent voting members of the governing body (Part VI, line 1b)

  

  

  

  
5

  
Total number of individuals employed in calendar year (Part V, line 2a)

  

  

  
  
Revenue

  

  
1

  
Contributions, gifts, grants, and similar amounts received

  

  

  

  

  
2

  
Program service revenue including government fees and contracts

  

  

  

  

  
3

  
Investment income (Part VIII, column (A), lines 3, 4, and 7d)

  

  

  

  

  
4

  
Other revenue (Part VIII, column (A), line 12)

  

  

  

  

  
5

  
Financial asset recognition / property interest income (accrual method)

  

  

  

  

  
12

  
Total revenue - add lines 1\'9611 (must equal Part VIII, column (A), line 12)

  

  

  

  
  
Expenses

  

  
13

  
Grants and other assistance to domestic organizations (Part IX, col. (A), line 1)

  

  

  

  

  
14

  
Benefits paid to or for members

  

  

  

  

  
15

  
Salaries, other compensation, employee benefits (Part IX, col. (A), lines 5\'9610)

  

  

  

  

  
17

  
Other expenses (Part IX, column (A), lines 11a\'9611d, 11f\'9624e)

  

  

  

  

  
18

  
Total expenses - add lines 13\'9617 (must equal Part IX, col. (A), line 25)

  

  

  

  

  
19

  
Revenue less expenses - subtract line 18 from line 12

  

  

  

  
  
Net Assets or Fund Balances (Part X)

  

  
20

  
Total assets (Part X, line 16)

  

  

  

  

  
21

  
Total liabilities (Part X, line 26)

  

  

  

  

  
22

  
Net assets or fund balances (subtract line 21 from line 20)

  

  

  

  
  
Sign Here - Under penalties of perjury, I declare that I have examined this return\'85

Date ________________

Form 1120 - U.S. Corporation Income Tax Return

  
Year-accurate format \'b7 EEON Foundation \'b7 Voluntary filing position per Glenshaw Glass, 348 U.S. 426 (1955)

  

  
  
  
  

  

  

  

  
Form 1120-W

  
Estimated Tax for Corporations \'b7 Installment Schedule

  

  
  
  

  

  

  
Form 1120-W

  
Department of the Treasury
Internal Revenue Service

  
OMB No. 1545-0175
20

  

  

  
Estimated Tax for Corporations

  
(Keep for the corporation's records - Do not send to the IRS)

  Go to www.irs.gov/Form1120W for instructions and the latest information.

  

  

  Name: 
  EIN: 
  

  
Part I Estimated Tax Computation

  
1

  

  Taxable income expected for the tax year
  
  

  

  
2

  

  Estimated income tax (21% of line 1)
  
  

  

  
5

  

  Estimated alternative minimum tax
  
  

  

  
6

  

  Estimated base erosion minimum tax amount
  
  

  

  
7

  

  General business credit (Form 3800)
  
  

  

  
8

  

  Total estimated tax (subtract line 7 from line 6)
  
  

  

  
Part II Installment Payments - Four equal installments

  

  
1st Installment
April 15

  
2nd Installment
June 15

  
3rd Installment
September 15

  
4th Installment
December 15
